chief_complaint: my period cramps are so bad I feel faint

med_suggestions.dosing: 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.sig: 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.notes: NSAIDs are first-line for dysmenorrhea if no contraindications.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.

med_suggestions.dosing: 500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.sig: 500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)
med_suggestions.exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.

med_suggestions.dosing: 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
med_suggestions.sig: 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
med_suggestions.notes: Use when NSAIDs contraindicated; may be less effective alone.
med_suggestions.exclusions: Severe liver disease; heavy alcohol use; allergy.

note.soap:
S: 
23-year-old female reports severe menstrual cramps occurring during her period. She describes the pain as so intense that she feels faint. She is soaking through pads very quickly, indicating heavy menstrual bleeding. She reports no fever, abnormal discharge, or ectopic pregnancy red flags. She has no medication allergies and notes reduced kidney function as a medication exclusion. The symptoms have a severe functional impact. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only history.

A: 
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Severe primary dysmenorrhea with heavy menstrual bleeding 
 - Differential: 
 1. Secondary dysmenorrhea (e.g., endometriosis, fibroids) 
 2. Coagulopathy or bleeding disorder contributing to heavy bleeding 
 3. Pelvic infection (less likely given no fever or discharge) 
 4. Structural uterine abnormalities (e.g., polyps, adenomyosis) 
 - Red flags considered: 
 - Heavy bleeding soaking through pads quickly (potential anemia risk) 
 - Feeling faint (possible hypovolemia or anemia) 
 - No ectopic pregnancy signs reported 

P: 
 - Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended due to heavy bleeding and faintness to assess for anemia, hemodynamic stability, and underlying causes. 
 - Plan: 
  - Primary medication option (based on your answers): Acetaminophen — 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
   Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
   - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
   - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
   - Ibuprofen — 400–600 mg PO q6–8h PRN with food (max per labeling; short course)
     - Notes: NSAIDs are first-line for dysmenorrhea if no contraindications.
     - Exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.
   - Naproxen — 500 mg PO once, then 250 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling; short course)
     - Notes: Alternative NSAID option.
     - Exclusions: Pregnancy (avoid esp 3rd trimester); renal disease; ulcer/GI bleed risk; anticoagulation; NSAID allergy.
   - Acetaminophen — 650–1000 mg PO q6–8h PRN (max per labeling)
     - Notes: Use when NSAIDs contraindicated; may be less effective alone.
     - Exclusions: Severe liver disease; heavy alcohol use; allergy.
   - Supportive care:
   - Non-pharmacologic: Heat pack to the lower abdomen, gentle activity/stretching as tolerated, hydration, and rest.
   - Medication safety: If you have kidney disease/reduced kidney function, stomach ulcers/GI bleeding, or take blood thinners, avoid NSAIDs like ibuprofen/naproxen unless a clinician specifically says it is safe for you.
   - Pregnancy safety: If there is any chance of pregnancy or your bleeding/pain is unusual for you, you may need pregnancy testing rather than assuming typical period cramps.
   - Follow-up and safety:
   - Re-evaluation: If severe pain is new, progressively worsening, associated with heavy bleeding, syncope, fever, pelvic pain between cycles, or concern for secondary dysmenorrhea—needs evaluation.
   - Urgent/ED now: Fainting, severe one-sided pelvic pain, suspected pregnancy/ectopic, soaking >1 pad/hour, severe weakness, or signs of shock.
 - Advise patient to seek emergency care if she experiences worsening dizziness, syncope, chest pain, or shortness of breath. 
 - Recommend over-the-counter analgesics such as acetaminophen or NSAIDs if kidney function permits; avoid NSAIDs if contraindicated due to reduced kidney function. 
 - Consider referral for gynecologic evaluation including pelvic ultrasound and laboratory tests (CBC, coagulation studies). 
 - Safety net: 
 - Instruct patient to go to the emergency department if bleeding worsens, she becomes unable to stand, or experiences severe weakness or chest pain. 
 - Follow up with primary care or gynecology within the next few days for further assessment and management.
patient_instructions: Non-pharmacologic: Heat pack to the lower abdomen, gentle activity/stretching as tolerated, hydration, and rest.

Medication safety: If you have kidney disease/reduced kidney function, stomach ulcers/GI bleeding, or take blood thinners, avoid NSAIDs like ibuprofen/naproxen unless a clinician specifically says it is safe for you.
Pregnancy safety: If there is any chance of pregnancy or your bleeding/pain is unusual for you, you may need pregnancy testing rather than assuming typical period cramps.
Re-evaluation: If severe pain is new, progressively worsening, associated with heavy bleeding, syncope, fever, pelvic pain between cycles, or concern for secondary dysmenorrhea—needs evaluation.
Urgent/ED now: Fainting, severe one-sided pelvic pain, suspected pregnancy/ectopic, soaking >1 pad/hour, severe weakness, or signs of shock.
